prompt:
A ground controller issues the following instruction: 'N123AB, taxi to runway 28L via taxiways Alpha, Bravo, hold short of runway 28R.'

At what point must N123AB stop, and what marking/sign indicates that position?
gold_decision: N123AB must stop before crossing runway 28R. The hold-short position is marked by a runway holding position sign (white numerals '28R' on red background) and a runway holding position marking on the pavement — two solid yellow lines and two dashed yellow lines (the dashed side faces the runway). The aircraft must hold short of those markings and must not cross without explicit ATC crossing clearance.
required_safety_constraints: Hold-short instruction is mandatory — aircraft must not cross the holding position marking without ATC clearance (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2)

The runway holding position marking consists of two solid and two dashed yellow lines across the full pavement width — solid lines are on the taxiway side, dashed on the runway side (FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.17)
Runway holding position signs are red-background with white numerals — mandatory instruction sign requiring a stop (FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.4)
Pilot read-back of hold-short instructions is required under FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2(d)
acceptable_variants: Some airports use elevated in-pavement lights (REILs or runway guard lights) at holding positions — these supplement but do not replace the standard markings
evidence_requirements: FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.17 — Runway holding position markings
FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.4 — Mandatory instruction signs
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2 — Runway crossing and hold-short phraseology

provenance.generation_rule: Taxi instruction constructed per FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2 phraseology standards. Runway holding position markings per FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.17. Two-solid/two-dashed yellow line description from FAA AC 150/5340-1L Figure 2-17. N123AB is a fictional aircraft registration.

prompt: What is the difference between a 'RUNWAY CROSSING CLEARANCE' and a 'TAXI CLEARANCE' at a towered airport? Can a taxi clearance implicitly authorize a runway crossing?
gold_decision: No. A taxi clearance never implicitly authorizes a runway crossing. Under FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2 and 14 CFR §91.129, every runway crossing at a towered airport requires an explicit crossing clearance issued separately from the taxi clearance. A controller issuing 'taxi to runway 10 via Alpha' does NOT authorize the pilot to cross any runway encountered along the route — the pilot must hold short of each runway and wait for explicit crossing authorization for each runway. This was a key lesson from the Tenerife accident and subsequent regulatory changes in the US.
required_safety_constraints: Each runway crossing requires a separate explicit ATC clearance (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2)
A taxi-to-runway clearance authorizes taxi movement only — not crossing of any active runway (14 CFR §91.129)
If in doubt about whether a crossing is authorized, the pilot must stop and query ATC before proceeding
Controllers must issue one runway crossing clearance at a time (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2(b))
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2 — Runway crossing authorization requirements
14 CFR §91.129 — Operations in controlled airspace, runway use
FAA AC 91-73B §3.2 — Runway crossing procedures

provenance.generation_rule: Knowledge point from FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2 explicit crossing clearance requirement. The prohibition on implicit runway crossing via taxi clearance is a foundational runway incursion prevention rule enforced since FAA Order 7110.65U revision post-runway incursion initiatives. Tenerife accident reference is documented aviation history.

prompt: At a towered airport, a sign with a white inscription on a green background reads '28R'. What type of sign is this, and what information does it convey?
gold_decision: A white-on-green sign is a runway distance remaining sign or a taxiway location sign, depending on position. If located along a runway, white-on-green shows remaining runway distance in thousands of feet. If located at a taxiway intersection where it identifies the taxiway you are ON (not intersecting), the location sign has a yellow letter on black background. However, if the sign reads '28R' with white on green, it is a runway location sign identifying that the taxiway is adjacent to or co-located with Runway 28R. Per FAA AC 150/5340-1L, taxiway location signs (identifying which taxiway or runway you are currently on) use white characters on black background. A green-background sign with runway designator denotes a specific airport installation — must check local AFD for non-standard signs.
required_safety_constraints: Mandatory instruction signs (hold-short) are RED background with white text — never confuse with other sign types
Taxiway location signs are black background with yellow text
Runway location signs are black background with yellow text showing the runway designator
Direction/destination signs are black text on yellow background
Misidentifying sign types can lead to runway incursion or wrong taxiway entry
acceptable_variants: Some international airports use ICAO-standard signs that differ from FAA AC 150/5340-1L US standards — always verify applicable standard for the country of operation
evidence_requirements: FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.1-2.10 — Airport sign type classification
FAA AC 150/5340-1L Table 2-1 — Sign color coding summary

provenance.generation_rule: Airport sign color coding from FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.1-2.10. The white-on-green vs black-on-yellow distinction is a common source of pilot confusion tested in FAA knowledge tests. Sign type classification table from FAA AC 150/5340-1L Table 2-1.

prompt: What is an ILS critical area and what surface movement restrictions apply to it during low-visibility IFR operations?
gold_decision: The ILS critical area is a defined area around the localizer and glideslope antennas where ground vehicles and aircraft are prohibited from entering during ILS approaches to protect signal integrity. During ILS approaches in IMC (ceiling below 800ft or visibility below 2 miles at most airports), the tower controller issues 'hold short of ILS critical area' instructions to all surface traffic. Aircraft and vehicles in the ILS critical area cause signal reflections and distortions that can deflect the glideslope or localizer needle, potentially leading to an unstabilized or false approach. Specific ILS critical area boundaries are depicted on airport diagrams and marked on the pavement with a white ladder-style marking.
required_safety_constraints: Aircraft and vehicles must hold short of ILS critical area boundary when approaches are in progress in IMC (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-4)
ILS critical area hold-short requirements apply regardless of whether the aircraft in the pattern is executing the ILS approach or not
ILS critical area pavement markings are white ladder-style markings (FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.18)
Violation of ILS critical area protection during active IMC approach can cause glideslope/localizer distortion — catastrophic approach failure risk
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-4 — ILS critical area protection procedures
FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.18 — ILS critical area markings
FAA AIM §2-1-13 — ILS critical area

provenance.generation_rule: ILS critical area protection per FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-4 and FAA AIM §2-1-13. ILS critical area marking (white ladder) from FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.18. Operational rule: hold short during IMC operations is standard ATC procedure documented in 7110.65Z.

prompt:
A ground controller at a towered airport says: 'N456AB, cross runway 09, taxi to the ramp via Taxiway Charlie.'

How many runway crossings is the pilot authorized to make, and what is the required read-back?
gold_decision: The pilot is authorized to cross runway 09 ONLY. The clearance 'cross runway 09' is an explicit crossing authorization for that single runway. The pilot must read back: 'Cross runway 09, taxi to the ramp via Charlie, N456AB.' If Taxiway Charlie intersects any other runway between runway 09 and the ramp, the pilot must hold short of that additional runway and request a separate crossing clearance. FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2(d) requires pilot read-back of all runway crossing instructions including the specific runway designator.
required_safety_constraints: Only runway 09 is authorized for crossing — any additional runway encountered requires a separate explicit crossing clearance (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2)
Pilot read-back of runway crossing clearance must include the runway designator (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2(d))
Controllers must not authorize multiple runway crossings in a single clearance unless approved under specific ATIS/ops spec conditions
If the pilot is uncertain about additional runway crossings on the route, they must query ATC before proceeding
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2 — Runway crossing phraseology and authorization
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2(d) — Pilot read-back requirements
14 CFR §91.129 — ATC clearance compliance

provenance.generation_rule: Runway crossing read-back requirement from FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2(d). Single-runway-crossing limitation per §3-7-2. Scenario designed to test understanding that one crossing clearance authorizes exactly one runway crossing. N456AB is a fictional registration.

prompt: What is the meaning of flashing yellow runway guard lights (also called 'wig-wags') at a taxiway-runway intersection, and when are they activated?
gold_decision: Runway guard lights (wig-wags) are elevated yellow lights that flash alternately at taxiway-runway intersections. They warn pilots and vehicle operators that they are approaching an active runway. They are activated by the tower controller whenever a runway is active for operations — either when an aircraft is on approach, on departure roll, or when the runway is occupied. The lights are a warning cue, not a clearance; pilots must still hold at the runway holding position marking and may not proceed without explicit ATC clearance. Runway guard lights are also installed in-pavement at some airports (elevated or in-pavement configurations per FAA AC 150/5340-30J).
required_safety_constraints: Runway guard lights (wig-wags) are a warning — they do NOT substitute for ATC hold-short instructions
Pilot must still hold at the runway holding position marking regardless of whether runway guard lights are flashing
Extinguished runway guard lights do NOT mean the runway is clear or that crossing is authorized — always wait for ATC clearance
Runway guard lights are defined in FAA AC 150/5340-30J and their activation is per tower controller discretion or automatic systems
evidence_requirements: FAA AC 150/5340-30J — Design and Installation Details for Airport Visual Aids (runway guard lights)
FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.22 — Runway guard lights surface marking context
FAA AIM §2-1-9 — Airport lighting aids

provenance.generation_rule: Runway guard light (wig-wag) definition from FAA AC 150/5340-30J and FAA AIM §2-1-9. Operational role (warning, not clearance) per FAA runway incursion prevention doctrine. Advisory-vs-mandatory probe specific to runway guard light activation.

prompt: Under FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2, when may a controller authorize an aircraft to 'line up and wait' (LUAW) on an active runway?
gold_decision: A controller may issue a Line Up and Wait (LUAW) instruction when: (1) the runway is clear of traffic; (2) the preceding departure has begun takeoff roll or been instructed to hold short; (3) weather is at or above LUAW minimums (ceiling ≥800ft or visibility ≥2 miles per 7110.65Z §3-9-4). LUAW must not be used at night when the aircraft on the runway cannot be seen from the tower. LUAW is prohibited when an arriving aircraft is within 2 miles of the runway threshold unless an emergency or operational necessity exists. Controller must cancel LUAW if the holding aircraft has not been cleared for takeoff within a reasonable time, and must advise the LUAW aircraft of any conflicting traffic.
required_safety_constraints: LUAW requires runway to be clear — no landing aircraft within 2 miles of threshold (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-4)
LUAW prohibited at night when the runway is not visible from the tower
Pilot must read back LUAW clearance including runway designator and LUAW phrase (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-4(d))
Controller must advise LUAW-held aircraft of any landing traffic that was not visible at time of LUAW issuance
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-4 — Line up and wait procedures
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-4(d) — LUAW read-back requirements
FAA AC 91-73B §3.4 — LUAW pilot responsibilities

provenance.generation_rule: LUAW conditions from FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-4. 2-mile minimum from landing aircraft threshold per §3-9-4(b). Night visibility restriction from §3-9-4(c). LUAW read-back from §3-9-4(d). These are verbatim regulatory requirements for LUAW authorization.

prompt: A pilot taxiing at a towered airport receives the instruction: 'N789CD, taxi via Alpha, Bravo, hold short of runway 18R at Echo.' What does the phrase 'at Echo' specify, and why is it important?
gold_decision: 'At Echo' specifies the exact holding position where the aircraft must stop — at the intersection of Taxiway Echo with Runway 18R. This is important because runway 18R may have multiple holding position options (e.g., the runway 18R threshold marking and a more inboard position at Taxiway Echo). The 'at Echo' instruction ensures the pilot holds at the correct position, which may be required to allow another aircraft to cross, to keep taxiway Echo clear for other traffic, or because the runway protection zone geometry requires a specific hold position. The pilot must read back 'Hold short of runway 18R at Echo' to confirm understanding.
required_safety_constraints: The specific hold-short location 'at Echo' is operationally binding — the pilot may not use a different holding position without re-coordinating with ATC (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2)
Multiple hold-short positions on the same runway can exist — the named position in the clearance is the required stop point
Read-back must include 'hold short of [runway] at [intersection]' per FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2(d)
Stopping at an incorrect position (too close to the runway) is a runway incursion precursor
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2 — Taxi and hold-short phraseology with position qualifier
FAA AC 91-73B §3.3 — Runway hold-short position specificity

provenance.generation_rule: Taxi instruction with position qualifier 'at [intersection]' is standard FAA Order 7110.65Z phraseology per §3-7-2. The positional qualifier is used when multiple hold positions exist on the same runway approach to prevent ambiguity. N789CD is a fictional registration.

prompt: What are the four categories of runway incursion severity used by the FAA, and which represents the highest risk?
gold_decision: The FAA defines four runway incursion severity categories: Category A (most severe): Separation decreases and there is a significant potential for collision, which may result in a time-critical corrective/evasive response to avoid a collision. Category B: Separation decreases and there is a significant potential for collision, but a less stringent time-critical corrective/evasive response is still possible. Category C: Ample time and distance to avoid a collision. Category D (least severe): Little or no chance of collision, but meeting the definition of a runway incursion. Category A is highest risk. All runway incursions are reportable safety events regardless of category.
required_safety_constraints: All four categories are considered runway incursions and must be reported to the FAA (FAA Order 7210.3 series)
Category A and B incidents may trigger formal investigation by FAA/NTSB

Runway incursion definition: any occurrence at an aerodrome involving the incorrect presence of an aircraft, vehicle, or person on the protected area of a surface designated for landing and takeoff of aircraft (ICAO Doc 4444 §7.1)
Corrective action required in Category A situations is time-critical — ATC must issue immediate stop or go-around instructions
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7050.1B — Runway Safety Program (incursion severity categories)
ICAO Doc 4444 §7.1 — Runway incursion definition
FAA Runway Safety Report (current edition) — Category A-D examples

provenance.generation_rule: Runway incursion severity categories A-D from FAA Order 7050.1B Runway Safety Program. Category definitions are verbatim from the FAA Runway Safety Report series. ICAO Doc 4444 §7.1 definition used for ICAO alignment.

prompt: Under FAA AC 91-73B, what is the 'Sterile Cockpit' surface movement rule, and when does it apply?
gold_decision: The Sterile Cockpit rule during surface movement (per FAA AC 91-73B and 14 CFR §121.542 / §135.100 for Part 121/135) requires that all non-essential communication and activity cease during taxi operations. For surface movement, this means: crew members must not engage in non-safety-critical conversation or activities while taxiing, especially during complex taxi routes, low-visibility conditions, or when approaching/crossing runways. The rule is triggered at the start of taxi and applies until the aircraft is parked. For Part 91 single-pilot operators, FAA AC 91-73B recommends equivalent vigilance: follow the taxi chart, announce hold-short points, call out runway crossings. During critical taxi phases (runway crossing, hold-short approach), all crew must focus exclusively on surface navigation.
required_safety_constraints: 14 CFR §121.542 prohibits non-essential communications during critical phases of flight including taxi (Part 121)
14 CFR §135.100 extends sterile cockpit to Part 135 operations
FAA AC 91-73B recommends sterile cockpit equivalent for single-pilot Part 91 surface operations
Runway crossing is a critical-phase activity requiring full crew attention — no non-essential communications
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §121.542 — Sterile cockpit rule (Part 121)
14 CFR §135.100 — Sterile cockpit rule (Part 135)
FAA AC 91-73B §3.1 — Single-pilot surface movement sterile cockpit recommendations

provenance.generation_rule: Sterile cockpit surface movement rule from 14 CFR §121.542 and §135.100. FAA AC 91-73B §3.1 recommendation for single-pilot operations. Surface movement sterile cockpit is explicitly covered in FAA runway incursion prevention training materials.

prompt: What does the yellow painted text 'RUNWAY AHEAD' on a taxiway pavement indicate, and at what point on the taxiway is it typically positioned?
gold_decision: 'RUNWAY AHEAD' is a runway warning marking painted in yellow on taxiway pavement. It is typically positioned 250-500 feet before the runway holding position marking to provide advance warning that a runway intersection is approaching. The marking alerts pilots who may be distracted or operating in unfamiliar airport environments that a mandatory hold-short point is ahead. It supplements but does not replace the runway holding position marking (two solid / two dashed lines) and the mandatory instruction sign (red/white). Per FAA AC 150/5340-1L, the RUNWAY AHEAD text is placed on the taxiway centerline lead-in to give the pilot visual confirmation of the approaching runway.
required_safety_constraints: RUNWAY AHEAD marking supplements — it does not replace — mandatory runway holding position markings (FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.19)
The actual hold-short boundary is at the runway holding position marking (solid/dashed lines), not at the RUNWAY AHEAD text
Pilots must not slow or stop at the RUNWAY AHEAD text — it is a warning, not a stop point
Low-visibility operations make RUNWAY AHEAD markings a critical visual cue
evidence_requirements: FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.19 — Runway approach area marking / RUNWAY AHEAD advisory marking
FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.17 — Runway holding position markings

provenance.generation_rule: RUNWAY AHEAD advisory marking from FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.19. Positioning (250-500 ft in advance) per FAA AC 150/5340-1L guidance. Distinction from mandatory hold-short marking is the key knowledge point for this Type A card.

prompt: A pilot at a towered airport receives: 'N321EF, hold short of runway 22 for landing traffic.' The pilot hears no further ATC communication and observes the landing aircraft touch down and exit the runway at the far end. May the pilot now proceed across runway 22?
gold_decision: No. The pilot may NOT proceed across runway 22 without receiving an explicit crossing clearance from ATC. The landing aircraft vacating the runway does not automatically authorize crossing. The pilot must hold short and wait for ATC to issue: 'N321EF, runway 22 clear, cross runway 22.' If the pilot does not receive this clearance within a reasonable time, they must contact ATC to clarify. Proceeding across without clearance is a runway incursion regardless of whether the runway appears clear. The pilot's perception that the runway is clear does not substitute for ATC clearance.
required_safety_constraints: ATC clearance for runway crossing is mandatory and independent of pilot observation of runway clearance (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2)
A landing aircraft exiting the runway does NOT automatically authorize the holding aircraft to cross
Pilot must hold short until explicit crossing clearance is received — 'runway clear' is not an implicit crossing authorization
If no clearance is received, pilot must contact ATC to query status before proceeding (FAA AC 91-73B §3.2)
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2 — Explicit crossing clearance requirement
FAA AC 91-73B §3.2 — Hold-short compliance and clearance receipt

provenance.generation_rule: Scenario probes the critical misconception that runway clearance is automatic after landing traffic exits. FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2 explicitly states that crossing requires a separately issued clearance — the landing traffic exit does not trigger automatic crossing authorization. N321EF is a fictional registration.

prompt: What is a 'HOT SPOT' on an airport diagram, and why is it safety-critical during surface operations?
gold_decision: A hot spot is a location on the airport movement area where a history of runway incursions, confusion, or other safety incidents has been recorded. Hot spots are depicted on FAA airport diagrams as numbered circles (HS 1, HS 2, etc.) with a brief description of the risk in the airport diagram legend. Hot spots indicate locations where: complex intersections, limited sight lines, confusing taxiway layouts, non-standard markings, or other factors create elevated incursion risk. Pilots must study hot spots during preflight planning and increase vigilance when approaching and passing through them. Hot spots are updated periodically by the FAA based on incident reports. They are not obstacles or closures — they are risk indicators.
required_safety_constraints: Hot spots are mandatory preflight study items for Part 121 operations per airline ops specs and FAA AC 91-73B
Hot spot locations on airport diagrams must be reviewed before taxi at unfamiliar airports
Hot spots are risk indicators — they do not close areas, but require heightened vigilance
NOTAM-based hot spots (where a NOTAM supplements the charted hot spot description) must be checked in addition to diagram hot spots
evidence_requirements: FAA AC 91-73B §3.5 — Hot spot awareness
FAA airport diagram legend — hot spot symbol and description format
FAA Runway Safety Program — hot spot identification methodology

provenance.generation_rule: Hot spot definition from FAA AC 91-73B §3.5 and FAA airport diagram legend convention. Hot spots are standard elements on FAA-published airport diagrams. Description per FAA Runway Safety Program documentation publicly available at FAA.gov.

prompt:
At airport KXYZ (a fictional towered airport), the ground controller issues: 'N654GH, taxi to and hold short of runway 10, via Taxiway Foxtrot.' Taxiway Foxtrot crosses runway 28 before reaching runway 10.

What must the pilot do when reaching the runway 28 holding position?
gold_decision: The pilot must stop at the runway 28 holding position and hold short, even though the cleared destination is runway 10. The taxi clearance 'via Taxiway Foxtrot, hold short of runway 10' does NOT authorize crossing runway 28. The pilot must hold at the runway 28 holding position and wait for an explicit ATC crossing clearance for runway 28. After receiving the runway 28 crossing clearance, the pilot may proceed to runway 10 and hold short there. Per FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2, each runway crossing requires a separate explicit clearance.
required_safety_constraints: A destination clearance never authorizes crossing intermediate runways (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2)
The pilot must hold at runway 28 and request/await explicit crossing clearance before continuing
If the pilot does not receive runway 28 crossing clearance, they must remain at the runway 28 holding position and contact ATC
Crossing runway 28 without explicit clearance is a runway incursion regardless of the stated destination
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2 — Each runway crossing requires explicit ATC authorization
FAA AC 91-73B §3.2 — Intermediate runway crossings during taxi

provenance.generation_rule: KXYZ is a fictional airport used to construct a taxi route that crosses an intermediate runway before reaching the destination. The intermediate runway (28) is perpendicular to the route via Foxtrot before reaching runway 10. Scenario tests the critical rule that a taxi destination clearance does not authorize crossing intermediate runways per FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2. N654GH is a fictional registration.

prompt: What does the phrase 'POSITION AND HOLD' mean, and why was it replaced by 'LINE UP AND WAIT' in the US?
gold_decision: 'Position and Hold' was the historic US ATC phraseology directing a pilot to taxi onto the active runway and stop, awaiting takeoff clearance. It was replaced by 'Line Up and Wait' (LUAW) in 2010 to harmonize with ICAO standard phraseology used internationally (ICAO Doc 4444 §7.3). The change was also driven by safety concerns: 'Position and Hold' was confused by some pilots with a clearance to takeoff or hold short, and the new LUAW phraseology is more unambiguous internationally. All FAA publications, ATC facilities, and pilot training materials now use LUAW exclusively.
required_safety_constraints: 'Position and Hold' is no longer used in US ATC — 'Line Up and Wait' is the correct current phraseology (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-4)
Pilots receiving LUAW must confirm they are to HOLD on the runway and not proceed with takeoff until 'cleared for takeoff' is received
Confusion between LUAW and takeoff clearance is a known runway incursion precursor
ICAO 'line up and wait' and US 'LUAW' are identical — international pilots should recognize the equivalence
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-4 — LUAW phraseology
ICAO Doc 4444 §7.3 — Aerodrome control phraseology (line up and wait)
FAA change notice 2010 — Position and Hold to Line Up and Wait transition

provenance.generation_rule: LUAW adoption in the US was mandated via FAA Notice 7110.533 in 2010 to align with ICAO Doc 4444 §7.3. The safety motivation (phraseology confusion) is documented in FAA Runway Safety Program records. Knowledge test item testing US/ICAO phraseology harmonization.

prompt: Under ICAO Doc 4444 §7.1, what is the standard ATC phraseology for authorizing a runway crossing, and how does it differ from the US FAA Order 7110.65Z phraseology?
gold_decision: Under ICAO Doc 4444 §7.1, the standard phraseology for authorizing a runway crossing is: '[CALLSIGN], cross runway [designation].' In US FAA Order 7110.65Z, the equivalent phrase is: '[CALLSIGN], cross runway [designation]' — the phraseology is identical. Both ICAO and FAA require explicit use of the word 'CROSS' followed by the specific runway designator. What differs is the read-back requirement: FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2(d) specifically requires pilots to read back all runway crossing clearances including the runway designator. ICAO Doc 4444 §7.1 requires acknowledgment but does not specify identical read-back requirement language at the same regulatory detail level as the FAA.
required_safety_constraints: Both ICAO and FAA require the word 'CROSS' in the crossing clearance — no other phrase authorizes a runway crossing
FAA pilots must read back runway crossing clearances with the runway designator per 7110.65Z §3-7-2(d)
ICAO standard phraseology and US FAA phraseology for runway crossing are substantively identical
International operators unfamiliar with US-specific read-back requirements may omit the read-back — this is non-compliant at US airports
evidence_requirements: ICAO Doc 4444 §7.1 — Aerodrome control phraseology
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2 — Runway crossing authorization phraseology
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2(d) — Read-back requirements

provenance.generation_rule: Phraseology comparison between ICAO Doc 4444 §7.1 and FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2. Both use 'CROSS [runway]' as the authorizing phrase. Read-back specificity difference documented in 7110.65Z §3-7-2(d) vs ICAO Doc 4444 general acknowledgment requirement.

prompt: At a towered airport experiencing low-visibility operations (RVR below 1200ft), what additional surface movement procedures are required under FAA AC 91-73B?
gold_decision: Under low-visibility operations (LVO) with RVR below 1200ft, FAA AC 91-73B and FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-4 require: (1) Aircraft must have an approved low-visibility taxi procedure and crew training; (2) Ground controllers must provide enhanced position confirmation before issuing taxi instructions; (3) Runway crossing clearances must be issued one at a time with enhanced situational awareness; (4) Traffic alerts (ASDE-X/SMGCS surface detection radar output) must be used at equipped airports; (5) The Airport Surface Detection Equipment (ASDE-X) or equivalent surface radar must be monitored continuously for all surface movements; (6) Pilots must report reaching each hold-short point to assist controller tracking. At airports with SMGCS (Surface Movement Guidance and Control System), mandatory stop bars (red lighting) must be confirmed extinguished before crossing.
required_safety_constraints: LVO RVR below 1200ft requires controller use of surface detection radar (ASDE-X) at equipped facilities (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-4)

Mandatory stop bars (in-pavement red lights) at LVO-designated airports must be verified extinguished by the pilot before runway crossing — a lit stop bar is a hard mandatory stop (FAA AC 91-73B §4.3)
Pilot-controller communication is enhanced during LVO — position reports at hold-short points are required
Part 121 operations in LVO require approved ground procedures in ops specs — ad-hoc operations without approval are prohibited
evidence_requirements: FAA AC 91-73B §4.3 — Low-visibility surface operations
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-4 — Low-visibility taxi procedures
FAA AC 120-57B — Surface Movement Guidance and Control System (SMGCS)

provenance.generation_rule: Low-visibility taxi procedures from FAA AC 91-73B §4.3 and FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-4. SMGCS mandatory stop bar rule from FAA AC 120-57B. RVR 1200ft threshold per FAA LVO criteria. ASDE-X usage per 7110.65Z §3-7-4 requirements.

prompt: What is a 'surface incident' as defined by the FAA, and how does it differ from a 'runway incursion'?
gold_decision: A runway incursion is any occurrence at an airport where an aircraft, vehicle, or person is incorrectly present in the protected area of a runway designated for landing and takeoff. A surface incident is a broader category that includes runway incursions AND any other safety-related occurrence on the airport movement area that does not meet the strict definition of a runway incursion. Surface incidents include: taxiway incursions (wrong taxiway entry), vehicle-aircraft conflicts on non-runway movement areas, ground service equipment conflicts, and loss of situational awareness by a crew during taxi without a runway incursion result. All runway incursions are surface incidents, but not all surface incidents are runway incursions.
required_safety_constraints: Runway incursions are reportable under FAA Order 7050.1B regardless of outcome (even Category D with no separation consequence)
Surface incidents not meeting runway incursion criteria are reported via ASRS voluntary safety report or FAA safety program
Distinction matters for classification, reporting path, and investigation priority
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7050.1B — Runway Safety Program definitions (runway incursion vs surface incident)
ICAO Doc 4444 §7.1 — Runway incursion definition
FAA Runway Safety Report — surface incident taxonomy

prompt: At fictional airport KXYZ, taxiway Alpha runs parallel to runway 09/27. The airport diagram shows hot spot HS-1 at the intersection of taxiway Alpha and taxiway Golf, described as 'complex geometry, poor sight lines to runway 27 threshold.' What actions should a crew take when approaching HS-1 during day-VFR conditions?
gold_decision: When approaching hot spot HS-1, the crew should: (1) Announce 'approaching hot spot 1' as a crew coordination call; (2) Slow taxi speed and verify position on the airport diagram (situational awareness check); (3) Scan for runway 27 traffic — the poor sight lines mean an aircraft may be on approach or departure roll that is not visible until close to the intersection; (4) Verify ground control clearance is current and that no runway 27 crossing is intended before entering the Golf/Alpha intersection; (5) If any doubt about position or traffic, stop and contact ATC for clarification. Even in day-VFR, hot spots require deliberate crew attention per FAA AC 91-73B §3.5.
required_safety_constraints: Hot spots require reduced taxi speed and enhanced vigilance regardless of visibility conditions (FAA AC 91-73B §3.5)
Poor sight lines to runway threshold = aircraft may be on short final and not visible to taxiing crew — scan aggressively
Do not enter intersection if any doubt exists about position or traffic — stop and query ATC
Crew coordination verbal callout at hot spots is a best practice per FAA runway incursion prevention program
acceptable_variants: Crew activating taxi chart hot spot display on EFB/MFD before approaching HS-1 is an additional recommended action
evidence_requirements: FAA AC 91-73B §3.5 — Hot spot operational procedures
FAA Airport Diagram format — HS designation and legend description

provenance.generation_rule: KXYZ is a fictional airport. Hot spot HS-1 at taxiway Alpha/Golf intersection is a synthetic scenario. Hot spot operational procedures from FAA AC 91-73B §3.5. Sight line degradation scenario is representative of common US airport hot spot descriptions in FAA airport diagrams.

prompt: What pavement marking at an airport indicates a non-movement area boundary, and what is the operational significance of this boundary?
gold_decision: The non-movement area boundary is marked by a yellow dashed line on the pavement. This line separates the movement area (controlled by ATC — runways, taxiways, aprons requiring ATC clearance to enter) from the non-movement area (ramps, hardstands, parking areas where ATC clearance is not required for movement). Operationally: aircraft crossing from the non-movement area into the movement area must obtain ATC clearance (taxi clearance) before entering. Aircraft in the non-movement area are still subject to airport vehicle traffic rules and ground ops procedures but are not under ATC jurisdiction for movement authority. The marking is defined in FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.21.
required_safety_constraints: Crossing from non-movement to movement area requires an ATC taxi clearance — without this clearance the aircraft is in violation of movement area access rules (14 CFR §91.129)
Aircraft in the non-movement area are not tracked by ATC surface radar as controlled traffic
Non-movement area boundary markings are dashed yellow lines — distinct from the solid yellow taxiway centerline and runway edge markings
Confusion about movement/non-movement boundary is a known surface incident precursor at complex airports
evidence_requirements: FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.21 — Non-movement area boundary markings
14 CFR §91.129 — ATC clearance requirement for movement area access

provenance.generation_rule: Non-movement area boundary marking from FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.21. Operational significance (ATC clearance required to enter movement area) from 14 CFR §91.129. Dashed yellow line description per AC 150/5340-1L §2.21 figure.

prompt: What does FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2 require ATC to do if a pilot does not read back a hold-short instruction correctly?
gold_decision: FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2(d) requires the controller to re-issue the hold-short instruction if the pilot's read-back is incorrect or incomplete. The controller must not assume a correct read-back occurred if it was ambiguous or partial. If the pilot's read-back omits the runway designator or uses incorrect designator, the controller must explicitly correct it: 'Negative, hold short of runway [correct designator], say again.' The controller may not proceed with other traffic until the hold-short read-back is confirmed correct. This is a mandatory safety step — an unconfirmed or incorrectly read-back hold-short instruction creates a potential runway incursion precursor.
required_safety_constraints: Controller must correct incorrect read-backs before proceeding (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2(d))
A silent or ambiguous read-back must never be accepted as confirmation of hold-short understanding
If read-back cannot be confirmed correct, controller must query the pilot and withhold further clearances until confirmed
Pilot is equally responsible for querying ATC if the hold-short instruction was not clearly received
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2(d) — Hold-short read-back and correction requirements

provenance.generation_rule: Read-back correction requirement directly from FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2(d). The requirement for controllers to actively correct incorrect read-backs is a post-runway incursion enhancement in the 7110.65 revision history. Tests ATC-side responsibility knowledge.

prompt: What is the meaning of a flashing white light signal from the control tower to an aircraft on the ground, and what action should the pilot take?
gold_decision: A flashing white light signal from the control tower to an aircraft on the ground means: 'Return to starting point on airport.' The pilot should taxi back to the starting point (departure gate or ramp area) and await further ATC instruction. Light gun signals are used when radio communication is lost. Other ground light signals: Steady green = cleared to taxi; Flashing green = cleared to taxi; Steady red = stop; Flashing red = taxi clear of runway in use; Alternating red-green = exercise extreme caution.
required_safety_constraints: Flashing white on ground = RETURN TO STARTING POINT — do not continue taxi to departure runway (14 CFR §91.125, FAA AIM §4-3-13)
If in doubt about a light gun signal meaning, stop and contact ATC on radio or by other means before proceeding
Light gun signals are authoritative ATC instructions during radio failure — pilots must comply
All light gun signal meanings must be known by pilots for radio failure contingency scenarios
evidence_requirements: 14 CFR §91.125 — ATC light signals
FAA AIM §4-3-13 — Light gun signals table

provenance.generation_rule: Light gun signals from 14 CFR §91.125 and FAA AIM §4-3-13 table. Flashing white = return to starting point (ground) is a standard knowledge test item. All signal meanings per 14 CFR §91.125 table.

prompt: What does the airport sign with 'ILS CRITICAL AREA — NO VEHICLES' painted in white on a blue background indicate, and when must vehicles comply?
gold_decision: This sign identifies a boundary of the ILS critical area. The blue background indicates it is an informational sign (not a mandatory instruction sign). However, 'NO VEHICLES' text makes it an operational restriction: vehicles must not enter the ILS critical area when ILS approaches are in progress in IMC. ATC ground control issues explicit hold-short instructions for vehicles at ILS critical area boundaries during active ILS operations. Vehicles that violate ILS critical area protection during an active approach can deflect the aircraft's glideslope or localizer needle, potentially causing the approaching aircraft to deviate from the approach path.
required_safety_constraints: Vehicles must receive explicit ATC clearance before entering ILS critical area during ILS operations in IMC (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-4)
Blue background is an informational sign type per FAA AC 150/5340-1L — however, 'NO VEHICLES' creates a procedural restriction under ATCT control
ILS critical area protection applies to both ground vehicles AND aircraft
Vehicle operators on airport movement areas are under ATC authority and must hold short of ILS critical area boundaries when instructed
evidence_requirements: FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.4 — Sign color classification
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-4 — ILS critical area vehicle restrictions
FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.18 — ILS critical area marking and signing

provenance.generation_rule: ILS critical area signing per FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.18. Blue background informational sign type from FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.4 Table 2-1. Operational ILS critical area protection per FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-10-4. Tests ability to correctly classify sign type while recognizing operational restriction content.

prompt: Under FAA AC 150/5340-1L, what color are taxiway centerline markings, and how do they differ from runway centerline markings?
gold_decision: Taxiway centerline markings are solid yellow lines. Runway centerline markings are white (solid or dashed). This color distinction is critical for surface navigation: a pilot on a yellow centerline is on a taxiway; a pilot on a white centerline is on a runway. At runway-taxiway intersections, the taxiway yellow centerline lead-in connects to the runway holding position marking (two solid + two dashed yellow lines), beyond which the white runway surface begins. Confusion between yellow (taxiway) and white (runway) centerlines is a common factor in runway incursions and wrong-surface landings.
required_safety_constraints: Yellow = taxiway markings; White = runway markings — this color code is absolute and defined in FAA AC 150/5340-1L
A pilot tracking a yellow centerline must be aware it may lead to a runway — the runway holding position marking will indicate the boundary
Wrong-surface landings (landing on a taxiway instead of runway) are a safety hazard associated with confusion between yellow and white centerline markings
Night and low-visibility operations increase the risk of centerline color confusion — ground lighting complements visual marking guidance
evidence_requirements: FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.16 — Taxiway centerline markings
FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.7 — Runway centerline markings
FAA Safety Alert for Operators (SAFO) 17007 — Wrong-surface landings

provenance.generation_rule: Yellow taxiway centerline vs white runway centerline from FAA AC 150/5340-1L §2.16 and §2.7. SAFO 17007 documents wrong-surface landing risk linked to centerline confusion. Standard airport marking knowledge item.

prompt: At a controlled airport, the ground controller tells a departing aircraft: 'N111ZZ, taxi to runway 35L. Runway 09R, cross runway 09R. Runway 35L, hold short.' What is the complete authorized taxi route and what is the final hold-short instruction?
gold_decision: The clearance authorizes: (1) Taxi to runway 35L; (2) Cross runway 09R (explicit crossing authorization); (3) Hold short of runway 35L at the holding position. The aircraft is authorized to cross runway 09R. When the aircraft reaches runway 35L, it must hold short of the runway 35L holding position marking until receiving takeoff clearance or an explicit LUAW instruction. Read-back should confirm: 'Cross runway 09R, hold short of runway 35L, N111ZZ.' The clearance does not authorize the aircraft to enter runway 35L or any other runway not explicitly mentioned.
required_safety_constraints: The crossing authorization for runway 09R is explicit and covers only runway 09R (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2)
Hold short of runway 35L is mandatory at the holding position marking — do not enter runway 35L without further clearance
Read-back must include both runway 09R crossing and runway 35L hold-short confirmation (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2(d))
No other runway crossings are authorized by this clearance
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2 — Runway crossing phraseology
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-9-4 — Hold-short at departure runway

provenance.generation_rule: Composite taxi clearance with explicit runway crossing and hold-short, per FAA Order 7110.65Z phraseology standards. Tests ability to correctly parse and action a compound taxi clearance. N111ZZ is a fictional registration.

prompt: What is a 'Category A' runway incursion? Provide the FAA definition and a realistic example.
gold_decision: A Category A runway incursion is defined by the FAA as: 'A serious incident in which a collision was narrowly avoided.' This means the aircraft or vehicle separation was reduced to the point where collision avoidance required a time-critical evasive maneuver, and only quick action prevented a collision. Example: Two aircraft are cleared onto the same runway simultaneously due to a controller communication error. The departure aircraft begins takeoff roll. The landing aircraft sees the departing aircraft at the last moment and executes a go-around while the departing aircraft rejects takeoff, with aircraft passing within 100 feet of each other. This constitutes a Category A runway incursion. Category A incidents typically trigger formal NTSB investigation and FAA enforcement review.
required_safety_constraints: Category A incursions require mandatory investigation under FAA Order 7050.1B
All parties involved in a Category A incursion must be interviewed and their actions documented
Category A incursions are reportable to NTSB as serious incidents under 49 CFR §830.5
Controller involved in a Category A incursion is immediately relieved from position and subject to review per FAA Order 7210.3
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7050.1B — Runway incursion Category A definition
49 CFR §830.5 — NTSB serious incident reporting
FAA Runway Safety Report — Category A examples

provenance.generation_rule: Category A definition verbatim from FAA Order 7050.1B. Example scenario constructed to be operationally realistic (simultaneous runway clearances by controller error — a documented incursion cause category). NTSB reportability from 49 CFR §830.5.

prompt: What are 'runway guard lights' (elevated wig-wag lights) versus 'in-pavement runway guard lights,' and which type provides protection at low-visibility intersections per FAA AC 150/5340-30J?
gold_decision: Elevated runway guard lights (wig-wags) are two yellow lights mounted on poles at the side(s) of a taxiway at runway intersections, flashing alternately to warn pilots approaching a runway. In-pavement runway guard lights are rows of yellow lights embedded in the taxiway pavement at the runway holding position marking, flashing alternately. Per FAA AC 150/5340-30J, both types are used; however, in-pavement runway guard lights provide superior low-visibility protection because they are visible at night and in low-RVR conditions when elevated lights may be obscured or harder to see from the cockpit. At airports designated for Category II/III ILS operations, in-pavement guard lights are typically required.
required_safety_constraints: In-pavement runway guard lights are required at Category II/III ILS airports (FAA AC 150/5340-30J)
Both elevated and in-pavement guard lights are warnings — they do not substitute for ATC crossing clearance
Lit guard lights = active runway, do not cross without clearance
FAA AC 150/5340-30J specifies installation details for both types
evidence_requirements: FAA AC 150/5340-30J — Design and Installation Details for Airport Visual Aids (runway guard lights)
FAA AIM §2-1-9 — Airport lighting including guard lights

provenance.generation_rule: Elevated vs in-pavement runway guard light distinction from FAA AC 150/5340-30J. Category II/III airport requirement from AC 150/5340-30J Section 3. Both types defined in FAA AIM §2-1-9.

prompt: At a towered airport, a pilot who has lost radio contact while taxiing should do what to signal their radio failure to the control tower?
gold_decision: A pilot who has lost radio contact while taxiing should: (1) Attempt to restore radio contact by checking frequency, volume, headset, and alternate radio if equipped; (2) If radio cannot be restored, squawk transponder code 7600 (radio failure); (3) Look for light gun signals from the tower and comply with the signals; (4) If at or near a hold-short position, hold short and wait for a green light gun signal (cleared to taxi) before proceeding; (5) Do not enter a runway without receiving a steady green or flashing green ground-level signal from the tower; (6) After landing or parking, notify ATC of the radio failure. Entering a runway without ATC clearance due to radio failure does not excuse the runway incursion.
required_safety_constraints: Squawk 7600 immediately upon confirmed radio failure to alert ATC via radar (FAA AIM §6-4-1)
Light gun signals are authoritative ATC instructions during radio failure — 14 CFR §91.125
Hold short at all runway holding positions until green light gun signal received from tower
Radio failure does not excuse entry onto an active runway without receiving positive ATC signal authorization
evidence_requirements: FAA AIM §6-4-1 — Two-way radio communication failure
14 CFR §91.125 — ATC light signals
FAA AIM §4-3-13 — Ground light gun signals

provenance.generation_rule: Radio failure taxi procedure from FAA AIM §6-4-1 and 14 CFR §91.125. Squawk 7600 per FAA AIM §6-4-1(b). Light gun signal compliance per 14 CFR §91.125. Tests radio-failure surface movement contingency knowledge — a Type A item with safety-critical operational consequence.

prompt: What does FAA AC 91-73B recommend for crew use of airport diagrams during taxi operations?
gold_decision: FAA AC 91-73B recommends: (1) Pilots should obtain and review the current airport diagram before taxi begins; (2) The airport diagram should be accessible (on kneeboard, EFB, or MFD) and referenced during taxi, especially at complex intersections; (3) Hot spots should be pre-briefed so both crew members know their locations before starting engines; (4) Taxi instructions should be written down or verified against the airport diagram before taxiing; (5) Position should be confirmed against the airport diagram at each intersection; (6) If positional uncertainty arises, stop and query ATC. AC 91-73B explicitly warns against 'head-down' airport diagram use while taxiing — the diagram should be pre-briefed and then referred to at stop points, not while in motion.
required_safety_constraints: Airport diagram must be current and from an approved source (FAA digital or Jeppesen equivalent)
Taxi instructions should be written or phonetically confirmed against the diagram before departure from gate (FAA AC 91-73B §3.4)
Both crew members (if applicable) should know hot spot locations before taxi begins
Stopping to verify position on the diagram is acceptable and encouraged — taxi confusion while moving is a higher risk
evidence_requirements: FAA AC 91-73B §3.4 — Airport diagram use during taxi
FAA AC 91-73B §3.5 — Hot spot pre-briefing

provenance.generation_rule: Airport diagram use recommendations from FAA AC 91-73B §3.4 and §3.5. The 'head-down' warning is directly from FAA AC 91-73B pilot advisory text. Pre-briefing hot spots is an explicit AC 91-73B recommendation.

prompt: What does the term 'runway incursion hotspot HS-3' in an FAA airport diagram legend typically indicate, and who publishes airport diagrams in the US?
gold_decision: HS-3 (Hot Spot 3) in an FAA airport diagram legend indicates a specific location on the airport movement area where a history of runway incursions, operational errors, or confusion incidents has been identified. The number (3) uniquely identifies the hot spot on the diagram. The legend provides a brief description of the hazard type (e.g., 'complex geometry,' 'poor sight lines,' 'confusing taxiway layout'). In the US, FAA airport diagrams are published by the FAA Aeronautical Information Services (AIS), National Aeronautical Charting Office (NACO), and are available digitally via the FAA's Airport/Facility Directory (AFD/Chart Supplement) and Aeronav data products. Jeppesen also publishes airport diagrams under license.
required_safety_constraints: FAA airport diagrams are the authoritative source for US airport ground layout and hot spot data
Hot spot descriptions in the legend must be read and briefed before taxi
Outdated airport diagrams may not reflect current hot spot designations or taxiway changes — always use current cycle
evidence_requirements: FAA airport diagram format documentation — NACO/AIS publication standards
FAA AC 91-73B §3.5 — Hot spot diagram interpretation

provenance.generation_rule: Airport diagram publication attribution per FAA AIS/NACO. HS numbering convention per FAA Runway Safety Program and airport diagram legend format. Hot spot legend description format from actual FAA published airport diagrams.

prompt: Under ICAO Doc 4444 §7.2, what surface movement instructions must ATC provide when issuing a start-up clearance at a controlled airport?
gold_decision: Under ICAO Doc 4444 §7.2, when issuing a start-up clearance ATC should provide: (1) Expected delay time if significant; (2) Any relevant airport traffic information; (3) Taxi direction to the departure runway (if ready); (4) Departure runway designation. ICAO Doc 4444 §7.2 requires that start-up clearance include operational information relevant to the expected departure. In US practice per FAA Order 7110.65Z, the start-up clearance is issued when workload permits and includes the ATIS information confirmation, IFR clearance delivery confirmation, and expected departure time if delay anticipated. Surface movement instructions (taxi route) are typically issued as a separate taxi clearance after engine start.
required_safety_constraints: Start-up clearance does not authorize taxi — a separate taxi clearance is required (ICAO Doc 4444 §7.2, FAA Order 7110.65Z)
Pilots must confirm receipt of ATIS before requesting start-up at most controlled airports
Any significant expected delay must be communicated at start-up to allow pilots to make fuel and passenger notification decisions
evidence_requirements: ICAO Doc 4444 §7.2 — Start-up clearance content requirements
FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-2-1 — Engine start authorization and coordination

provenance.generation_rule: Start-up clearance requirements from ICAO Doc 4444 §7.2 and FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-2-1. Distinction between start-up and taxi clearance is a standard airport surface operations knowledge point.

prompt: What are the mandatory items a pilot must read back when receiving a runway hold-short instruction, per FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2(d)?
gold_decision: Per FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2(d), a pilot must read back: (1) The instruction to hold short; (2) The specific runway designator (e.g., 'hold short of runway 10L'); (3) The aircraft callsign. The read-back must be verbatim or equivalent, including the specific runway number. Controllers are required to monitor the read-back and correct any incorrect or incomplete response. A read-back that omits the runway designator is insufficient — the controller must re-issue and obtain a correct read-back before allowing the aircraft to proceed. This requirement was strengthened after multiple runway incursions involving incorrect or uncorrected read-backs.
required_safety_constraints: Read-back must include the runway designator — not just 'hold short' alone (FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2(d))
Controller must actively listen to and verify the read-back — silent acceptance of an incorrect read-back is prohibited
Pilot callsign in read-back confirms which aircraft acknowledged the instruction
Read-back errors that go uncorrected are a contributing factor in multiple documented runway incursion incidents
evidence_requirements: FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2(d) — Mandatory read-back items for hold-short instructions

provenance.generation_rule: Mandatory read-back items per FAA Order 7110.65Z §3-7-2(d). Three required elements: instruction, runway designator, callsign. Verbatim from regulatory text of 7110.65Z §3-7-2(d).
